elements.sent-2411-001: Machine Learning (ML) for software engineering (SE) has gained prominence due to its ability to significantly enhance the performance of various SE applications.
elements.sent-2411-002: This progress is largely attributed to the development of generalizable source code representations that effectively capture the syntactic and semantic characteristics of code.
elements.sent-2411-003: In recent years, pre-trained transformer-based models, inspired by natural language processing (NLP), have shown remarkable success in SE tasks.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, source code contains structural and semantic properties embedded within its grammar, which can be extracted from structured code-views like the Abstract Syntax Tree (AST), Data-Flow Graph (DFG), and Control-Flow Graph (CFG).
elements.sent-2411-005: These code-views can complement NLP techniques, further improving SE tasks.
elements.sent-2411-006: Unfortunately, there are no flexible frameworks to infuse arbitrary code-views into existing transformer-based models effectively.
elements.sent-2411-007: Therefore, in this work, we propose CodeSAM, a novel scalable framework to infuse multiple code-views into transformer-based models by creating self-attention masks.
elements.sent-2411-008: We use CodeSAM to fine-tune a small language model (SLM) like CodeBERT on the downstream SE tasks of semantic code search, code clone detection, and program classification.
elements.sent-2411-009: Experimental results show that by using this technique, we improve downstream performance when compared to SLMs like GraphCodeBERT and CodeBERT on all three tasks by utilizing individual code-views or a combination of code-views during fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-010: We believe that these results are indicative that techniques like CodeSAM can help create compact yet performant code SLMs that fit in resource constrained settings.
elements.sent-2411-011: Machine learning for software engineering (ML4SE) is an active area of research that has demonstrated substantial improvements in many SE tasks such as source code completion \citep{xu2022systematic , automatic code summarization \citep{zhang2022survey and semantic code search \citep{guo2020graphcodebert .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The success of these ML4SE pipelines largely depends on effectively learning source code representations.
elements.sent-2411-013: Hence, introducing novel ML techniques that incorporate information from source code is an active area of research \citep{10.1145/3212695 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: With the advent of language models, many works have explored methods to adapt natural language techniques to source code directly \citep{feng2020codebert, nijkamp2022codegen, xu2022systematic .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Although popular NLP techniques show impressive results when applied to source code \citep{feng2020codebert , various studies emphasize that source code is a richer construct and should not be treated simply as a collection of tokens or natural language text \citep{zhang2019novel .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: As a result, contemporary research in ML4SE deliberately avoids restricting itself to traditional NLP approaches alone.
elements.sent-2411-017: Instead, it strives to leverage program analysis techniques and code-views such as the AST, CFG, and DFG, and integrate them into language models to achieve a more comprehensive understanding of source code.
elements.sent-2411-018: This can be seen from transformer-based \citep{vaswani2017attention SLMs like GraphCodeBERT \citep{guo2020graphcodebert and SemanticCodeBERT \citep{du2023pre that are performant in multiple downstream SE tasks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: To accommodate graph-based code-views into a transformer model, works like GraphCodeBERT convert a code-view into a sequence and then append this sequence to the original code tokens.
elements.sent-2411-020: However, upon closer examination, we identify and elaborate on two major drawbacks of this approach - (1) the loss of structural information when flattening graphs and (2) the inability to scale this approach in a multi-code-view setting.
elements.sent-2411-021: Flattening Graphs: Since most code-views are predominantly graph structures, they cannot be directly used by models that consume sequential tokens (like transformers).
elements.sent-2411-022: As a result, most methods like GraphCodeBERT flatten the graph code-views by applying post-processing techniques like extracting all the graph's nodes into a sequence.
elements.sent-2411-023: Flattening graphs into sequences erodes the structural information contained in the edges of the graph code-view.
elements.sent-2411-024: To compensate for this loss, GraphCodeBERT attempts to learn the graph topology using a pre-training task of edge-prediction between the graph nodes.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, we note that it is important to prioritize an approach that takes the complete graph topology as input rather than focusing on mitigating information loss from graph flattening.
elements.sent-2411-026: Ballooning Input Size: After the graph is flattened into a sequence, approaches like GraphCodeBERT proceed to append this sequence to the original code snippet tokens.
elements.sent-2411-027: As most SLMs can typically handle sequence lengths of up to $1024$ or $2048$ tokens \citep{beltagy2020longformer , the original code tokens are often truncated to accommodate the additional code-view sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-028: This problem is further exaggerated when considering multiple code-views.
elements.sent-2411-029: Trying to accommodate the flattened sequences of two or more code-views would result in input overflow or extreme truncation of the original code tokens, resulting in the loss of crucial context.
elements.sent-2411-030: Hence, there is a need for a scalable method that incorporates code-views without increasing sequence lengths.
elements.sent-2411-031: Based on the above observations, we believe that existing methods fall short when it comes to effectively integrating multi-code-view representations into transformer models.
elements.sent-2411-032: To solve the above drawbacks, we propose CodeSAM, a novel approach to infuse information from multi-code-views by modifying the self-attention mechanism for transformers without altering the input token sequence.
elements.sent-2411-033: Additionally, after generating this self-attention mask, we propose two masking strategies for transformer models, that help incorporate local and global context in the source code representations.
elements.sent-2411-034: We also design CodeSAM to be code-view agnostic and applicable to any single or multi-code-view graph representation.
elements.sent-2411-035: Hence, we believe that CodeSAM is the first work to showcase how to execute a practical and complete pipeline - beginning from the static analysis of non-compilable code snippets, abstracting this analysis into a language and code-view agnostic format and finally integrating this format into transformer models without impacting input sequence lengths.
elements.sent-2411-036: This is unlike other works that implement methodologies that are specific to one code-view or methods that incorporate multiple code-views by significantly increasing input sequence length like GraphCodeBERT and SemanticCodeBERT.
elements.sent-2411-037: In Section \ref{sec:codeviewgeneration , we present a systematic analysis of the most relevant code-view generating tools often required for ML pipelines, and describe how we arrived at an appropriate tool to be plugged into our CodeSAM pipeline.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Since there exist many static analysis tools that are language specific \citep{kovalenko2019pathminer,bieber2022library , or code-view specific, without support for customizing or combining multiple code-views, we believe that this section will help future ML4SE researchers in selecting appropriate analysis tools.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Section \ref{sec:experiments deals with the evaluation of our methodology through experiments on code clone detection, semantic code search, and program classification.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Section \ref{sec:related_work contains the relevant related literature.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Section \ref{sec:threats entails the Threats to Validity of this work and is followed by Conclusion and Future Work in Section \ref{sec:conclusion .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: In this section, we introduce and explain CodeSAM - our approach to infuse multiple code-views without flattening its graph structure and simultaneously leaving the input token sequence untouched.
elements.sent-2411-043: We achieve this by choosing to carefully modify the model's attention mechanism based on the given custom (or combined) code-view.
elements.sent-2411-044: As this approach is essentially a masking procedure, it is applicable to any self-attention based model that consumes sequential data.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.65\textwidth]{images/Fig2.png} \caption{End-to-End System to infuse code-view information into Transformer models} \label{fig:system}
elements.sent-2411-045: The self-attention mechanism is an operation that computes a similarity measure between every pair of input tokens.
elements.sent-2411-046: During the course of training, the self-attention mechanism learns an attention matrix $A \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times N}$ where $N$ is the number of input tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The matrix $A$ can thus be viewed as a graph adjacency matrix connecting all tokens in the input.
elements.sent-2411-048: With this view in mind, it is possible to use insights from the highly structured graph-based code-views, to modify $A$ and influence the model's learning process.
elements.sent-2411-049: In this paper, we introduce CodeSAM that takes a code-view as input and translates it into an attention mask $A' \in \{0,1\}^{N \times N}$.
elements.sent-2411-050: By replacing $A$ with the Hadamard product $A' \odot A $ in the self-attention operation, we can force each token in the input sequence to attend to only those tokens in the code that are connected to it by a code-view.
elements.sent-2411-051: It is important to note that our approach has no effect on the input sequence to the model, whereas, adding sequences of flattened code-views dramatically increases input length.
elements.sent-2411-052: This is particularly advantageous for fixed (or short) length models like SLMs, where we often need to truncate the original code snippet to accommodate the flattened code-views.
elements.sent-2411-053: Additionally, CodeSAM can be easily applied to any code-view that is needed for specific downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-054: Our implemented pipeline can take any code-view as input, process this code-view to create sentence-level masks and subsequently translate this to the attention mask $A'$ (Fig. \ref{fig:system ).}
elements.sent-2411-055: In the following sections, we explain (i) BackSlice (Algorithm \ref{alg:backwardslice ) - a backward slicing algorithm that creates sentence level masks and (ii) AttentionGen - a method that converts these masks to the attention mask ($A'$).. (i) BackSlice.}

elements.sent-2411-057: We represent a code snippet ($CS$) as a list of statements - $\{S_1,S_2,\cdots,S_M\}$ where $M$ is the total number of lines.
elements.sent-2411-058: We refer to the generated code-views using the following naming convention - we denote the AST as $\mathbb{T _{A}$, the statement level DFG as $\mathbb{T _{D}$ and the statement level CFG as $\mathbb{T _{C}$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-060: For example, $\mathbb{T _{A+D}$ is the combination of $\mathbb{T _{A}$ and $\mathbb{T _{D}$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-093: Various works involving ML for code require generating different code-view graphs such as the AST \citep{alon2019code2vec, alon2020structural, kim2021code, wang2021code, zhang2019novel, zügner2021languageagnostic , CFG \citep{bieber2020learning, bieber2022static, defreez2018pathbased , DFG \citep{guo2020graphcodebert, kaufman2021learned, gao2023code , etc. Some also rely on graphs that combine information from various code-views \citep{vasudevan2021learning, du2023pre .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Hence, static analysis tools for code-view generation are popularly used by researchers in ML4SE.
elements.sent-2411-095: However, with such a plethora of tools available, it is crucial to identify the most suitable one for the specific ML dataset and approach at hand.
elements.sent-2411-096: In what follows, we elaborate on how we compare different tools and then select our tool of choice, which we integrate into the CodeSAM pipeline (Fig. \ref{fig:system ).}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Satisfying the Code-View requirements of CodeSAM
elements.sent-2411-097: In this section, we identify and discuss some common characteristics of widely used ML datasets.
elements.sent-2411-098: Based on these characteristics we list down certain requirements that the chosen static analysis tool should ideally support.
elements.sent-2411-099: Typically, ML approaches for various SE tasks such as semantic code search, code clone detection, and program classification, rely on large datasets consisting of partial/non-compilable code snippets.
elements.sent-2411-100: Often, these large datasets are method-level datasets \citep{codesearchnet, svajlenko2014towards consisting of individual methods with incomplete information about the source of some external variables and functions.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Even in the case of file-level datasets \citep{ibmcodenet , as each file-level data point is analysed outside of a project, it may have external dependencies which are not resolvable.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Hence, regular static analysis tools that need the code to be compilable cannot be used for analyzing such ML datasets.
elements.sent-2411-103: For example, SOOT \citep{soot , one of the most popular static analysis tools for Java, needs the Java source code to be compilable and for all definitions to be available because it compiles and converts the code into an intermediate representation before performing analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-104: Hence, this approach does not work for most popular ML datasets, including the ones we use for our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-105: Thus, the chosen tool must perform static analysis directly on the source code by using reasonable approximations where accurate analysis isn't possible.
elements.sent-2411-106: We discuss on three open source tools we found that work in this setting - (i) COMEX \citep{comex , (ii) Joern \citep{yamaguchi2014modeling and (iii) python-graphs \citep{bieber2022library .}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: As we experiment solely on Java datasets, python-graphs is not relevant to the experiments in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-108: Additionally, we note that COMEX is a generic tool aimed at generating customizable combined code-view representations, while Joern is a specialized tool primarily designed to aid in vulnerability detection.
elements.sent-2411-109: ML datasets can also include file-level data points \citep{ibmcodenet .}
elements.sent-2411-110: Thus, the chosen tool should support both inter-procedural (file-level) and intra-procedural (method-level) analysis.
elements.sent-2411-111: Upon evaluating COMEX, we find that it supports out-of-the-box inter-procedural analysis using approximations to alias analysis.
elements.sent-2411-112: In comparison, Joern has limited support for inter-procedural analysis, forcing previous works to re-implement many program-analysis components \citep{li2019comparative, pewny2016evilcoder .}
elements.sent-2411-113: In order to perform an exhaustive analysis, the chosen tool should be able to generate, customize and combine various code-views with ease.
elements.sent-2411-114: This is non-trivial since the tool would have to output each code-view in a generalised format, making it possible to identify and combine common nodes across these representations.
elements.sent-2411-115: As a result it is possible to find many tools that focus on generating a single code-view, but it is difficult to find tools that have support for generating and combining multiple code-views.
elements.sent-2411-116: For example, Astminer\citep{kovalenko2019pathminer (previously PathMiner) can generate an AST and a submodule in GraphCodeBERT can generate a DFG, but neither can generate combined code-views.}
elements.sent-2411-117: COMEX and Joern, on the other hand, can provide multiple code-views.
elements.sent-2411-118: COMEX can generate any customized combination of code-views from AST, CFG and DFG.
elements.sent-2411-119: Joern allows Code Property Graph (CPG) generation from a wide range of languages and implements its own query language to work with the same.
elements.sent-2411-120: The CPG is a combination of the control dependence graph (CDG), data dependence graph (DDG) and the AST.
elements.sent-2411-121: Despite allowing extraction of individual code-views, Joern currently lacks out-of-the-box support for easily extracting arbitrary combinations like ``CDG and AST" or ``DDG and AST".
elements.sent-2411-122: If such combinations are required, the user has to filter out the required information from the CPG.
elements.sent-2411-123: In addition to the above requirements, we note that it is relatively simpler to implement the CodeSAM algorithm when the static analysis tool provides control-flow and data-flow edges between statements rather than producing it at a token level.
elements.sent-2411-124: Contrasting between COMEX and Joern, COMEX has a statement-level output, whereas Joern outputs data-flow edges between AST leaf-nodes and control-flow edges between intermediate AST nodes.
elements.sent-2411-125: Based on the above observations, we integrate COMEX into the CodeSAM pipeline as it provides the most flexibility and the most ease of use.
elements.sent-2411-126: Additionally, COMEX is built on tree-sitter\footnote{https://tree-sitter.github.io/tree-sitter/ - an incremental parser that can parse syntactically incomplete code, and provides a uniform parsing interface for over $40$ programming languages.}
elements.sent-2411-127: Thus, as and when COMEX is extended to support a new language, this also increases the coverage for the CodeSAM pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-128: As of today, COMEX provides both method-level and file-level support for Java and C\#.
elements.sent-2411-129: Other than the tools mentioned in this section, we also compare COMEX against GraphCodeBERT's \citep{guo2020graphcodebert program analysis module for the sake of completeness.}
elements.sent-2411-130: It is important to note that, unlike COMEX and Joern, the GraphCodeBERT module is not a full-fledged tool.
elements.sent-2411-131: Rather, it is a collection of scripts that compute the data-flow graph for six programming languages.
elements.sent-2411-132: We describe a detailed comparison between COMEX and this module in Section \ref{sec:gcb-compare .}

elements.sent-2411-140: We highlight one issue with the help of a for-loop construct spanning Lines $5-12$.
elements.sent-2411-141: In this for-loop, the variable (j) used in the condition at Line $5$, is also modified in the body of the loop at Line $8$.

elements.sent-2411-144: The GraphCodeBERT implementation also exhibits evident deficiencies in correctly handling scope and lacks inter-procedural analysis, among other shortcomings.
elements.sent-2411-145: Thus, we conclude that GraphCodeBERT's approximate analysis, while suitable for its approach, would not suffice our requirements.
elements.sent-2411-146: COMEX, on the other hand, facilitates the generation of a statement-level DFG using RDA\footnote{Reaching Definition Analysis is a pivotal technique in data flow analysis \citep{aho2007compilers .}}
elements.sent-2411-147: Its primary objective is to determine the set of definitions that have the potential to impact the value of a variable at a specific program point.
elements.sent-2411-148: Although an RDA-based analysis inherently incurs higher computational costs, as compared to non-RDA analysis (like GraphcodeBERT), we opt for COMEX to ensure that we use more accurate code-view graphs.

elements.sent-2411-149: Customizations used in CodeSAM In addition to generating the typical code-views like AST, CFG and DFG, COMEX also provides options to further customize these views, which proves extremely useful when integrating COMEX into CodeSAM.
elements.sent-2411-150: In particular, we exhaustively experiment with the ``Last Def" and ``Last Use" customization options that are available for the data-flow graph, dedicating separate results (with these options enabled) in each table in Section \ref{sec:experiments .}
elements.sent-2411-151: We notice that the ``Last Def" and ``Last Use" options ensure a more comprehensive coverage of variable dependencies for those code snippets that refer to many global variables and functions that are out-of-context or undefined.
elements.sent-2411-152: We depict this with the help of an example in Fig \ref{fig:last_def .}
elements.sent-2411-153: As shown, the graph in Fig. \ref{fig:last_def is a combination of the CFG, DFG, Last Use and Last Def edges for the code snippet in Listing \ref{lst:last_def .}}
elements.sent-2411-154: The ``Last Use" relationship establishes links between the current usage of a variable and the program point where it was most recently read.
elements.sent-2411-155: Thus, the green edge from Line $3$ to Line $4$ is a ``Last Use" edge.
elements.sent-2411-156: This allows us to track the global variable ``currentBatchSize" which would have otherwise been ignored by an RDA-based analysis due to its missing declaration.
elements.sent-2411-157: With a similar motivation, we also enable the ``Last Def" relationship, which introduces extra edges that connect re-definitions of variables, allowing for the inclusion of edges between variable declarations and their corresponding definitions.
elements.sent-2411-158: The yellow edge from Line $5$ to Line $9$ in Fig. \ref{fig:last_def is a ``Last Def" edge.}
elements.sent-2411-159: Just as before, the ``preparedStatement" variable has not been declared in the scope of the provided code snippet.
elements.sent-2411-160: However, there exists a definition on Line $5$ and hence the closest viable definition is denoted as a data flow edge.
elements.sent-2411-161: Like ``Last Use", the ``Last Def" data-flow relationships thus prove particularly valuable in method-level snippets that heavily rely on global variables, which are not explicitly defined within the method body.

elements.sent-2411-163: To study the effectiveness of our approach, we run our end-to-end system (Fig. \ref{fig:system ), and feed different attention masks into the pre-trained transformer model CodeBERT \citep{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2002.08155 .}}
elements.sent-2411-164: CodeBERT is a bimodal transformer for programming and natural languages.
elements.sent-2411-165: It has been extensively trained on the considerably large ($\sim2.3$ million data points) CodeSearchNet corpus \citep{codesearchnet using masked language modelling (MLM) and replaced token detection \citep{feng2020codebert .}}
elements.sent-2411-166: In our experiments, rather than pre-training a deep learning language model from scratch (which is compute-intensive), we fine-tune CodeBERT with the attention mask $A'$ on the downstream tasks of semantic code search, program classification and code clone detection.
elements.sent-2411-167: It is important to note that most transformer models (like CodeBERT) have multiple layers in their architecture, where each layer has its own self-attention operation.
elements.sent-2411-168: Hence, we can choose to apply $A'$ to every layer or selectively apply it to certain layers.
elements.sent-2411-169: In this work we experiment with two options - (a) All Layer Masking: Applying $A'$ to every layer and (b) Alternate Layer Masking: Applying $A'$ to every alternate layer in the model.
elements.sent-2411-170: Additionally, in certain code snippets, we observe that the masking percentage is exceedingly high (say $> 90\%$).
elements.sent-2411-171: This is because the code snippet refers to a lot of out-of-context information like undefined or global variables, or a myriad of method invocations on undefined objects.
elements.sent-2411-172: In such code snippets, $A'$ is very sparse, masking out a lot of information.
elements.sent-2411-173: In these scenarios, it is prudent to avoid masking in the model and instead let every token attend to all other tokens.
elements.sent-2411-174: Hence, we also perform some ablations with different masking limits ($70\%, 80\%, 90\%$).
elements.sent-2411-175: Using the above techniques, we additionally experiment with different code-view combinations and model ensembles to determine what works best for each task.
elements.sent-2411-176: In what follows, we first elaborate on some common architectural components and then explain our results for the downstream applications of semantic code search, program classification and code clone detection.
elements.sent-2411-177: Before going into the task-specific architectures, we first describe in detail two major components used in all of our pipelines - (a) Input Preprocessor (Fig. \ref{fig:input_preprocessor ) and (b) MCV Encoder (Fig. \ref{fig:mcv_encoder ).}}
elements.sent-2411-178: As shown in Fig. \ref{fig:input_preprocessor , the Input Preprocessor is responsible for code snippet tokenization and attention mask generation ($A'$).}
elements.sent-2411-179: For code snippet tokenization, we run the tree-sitter parser on the code snippet and collect a list of leaf tokens as the input.
elements.sent-2411-180: This list is then further tokenized using CodeBERT's tokenizer resulting in a list of Input IDs.
elements.sent-2411-181: For the attention mask creation, we run Algorithm \ref{alg:backwardslice on the input code snippet and generate $A'$.}
elements.sent-2411-182: Once the code is tokenized and $A'$ is generated, we feed this to MCV Encoder (Fig. \ref{fig:mcv_encoder ) to generate a semantic vector representation of the code snippet.}
elements.sent-2411-183: Based on the task, these vector representations are used to solve the different problems of search, classification and clone detection.
elements.sent-2411-184: In what follows, we describe in depth the pipeline for each task and our associated experimental results.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/Fig8.png} \label{fig:semantic_code_search} \caption{Pipeline Diagram for Semantic Code Search. (Input Preprocessor and MCV Encoder detailed in Fig. \ref{fig:input_preprocessor} and Fig. \ref{fig:mcv_encoder})}
elements.sent-2411-185: Semantic code search is the task of retrieving a relevant code snippet from a code-base, given a natural language query.
elements.sent-2411-186: As software systems increasingly embrace open-source models, developers now have greater access to a vast array of source code.
elements.sent-2411-187: This accessibility allows developers to search for and utilize code snippets that can address similar (if not identical) tasks \citep{Di_Grazia_2023 , thereby improving productivity and reducing development time.}
elements.sent-2411-188: By solving the problem of semantic code search, we aim to develop techniques that aid in this search process.
elements.sent-2411-189: It is important to note that code search has two modalities - natural language and code.
elements.sent-2411-190: Code, unlike natural language, is structured data and has unambiguous semantics.
elements.sent-2411-191: This nature of code poses a problem, as traditional search engines are trained to capture semantics from unstructured data (like text) and are hence not effective at code retrieval tasks \citep{10.1145/3196398.3196425 .}
elements.sent-2411-192: To solve this problem, most semantic code search related works harness not only the natural language query but also the unique code structure to effectively retrieve the most relevant results.
elements.sent-2411-193: The text above the function definition is the documentation corresponding to the code snippet below it.. , firstnumber=1,language=iJava]{code_snippets/search_snippet.java.}
elements.sent-2411-194: We evaluate our performance on this task using the CodeSearchNet Java corpus.
elements.sent-2411-195: This dataset was created by extracting pairs of documentation and code implementation from thousands of open-sourced GitHub repositories.
elements.sent-2411-196: We present an example from CodeSearchNet in Listing \ref{fig:codesearch:code_snippet .}
elements.sent-2411-197: As seen, the data point has a method-level summary along with a code implementation.
elements.sent-2411-198: Unfortunately, some data points in CodeSearchNet are of poor quality.
elements.sent-2411-199: Thus, we reuse the modified version of this dataset as proposed by \citep{guo2020graphcodebert , wherein low-quality queries are removed using handcrafted rules.}
elements.sent-2411-200: These handcrafted rules target those data points with excessively short documentation or code implementation.
elements.sent-2411-201: The original dataset setting \citep{husain2020codesearchnet restricts the number of candidate code snippets, during inference, to $1000$.}
elements.sent-2411-202: This limitation does not reflect the true complexity of this task.
elements.sent-2411-203: Therefore, we follow \citep{ guo2020graphcodebert and expand the total number of candidate code snippets to the entire code base.}

elements.sent-2411-205: Whereas, the test and the development datasets contain only $5,183$ and $10,955$ natural language queries (documentation) that are used to retrieve code from a code base with $40,347$ code snippets.

elements.sent-2411-206: To adapt MCV Encoder for code search, we first perform fine-tuning with a contrastive loss function and then perform inference on the test set using the entire codebase.
elements.sent-2411-207: Fine-tuning: The fine-tuning process in code search involves training the model to correctly identify matching pairs of code snippets and natural language descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-208: For this, we use a contrastive loss function, wherein, for every mini-batch, we obtain one set of embeddings for each natural language query and another set for each code snippet.
elements.sent-2411-209: Then we compute the dot product between these two sets of vectors, trying to maximize the dot product between matching pairs and minimizing the dot product between others.
elements.sent-2411-210: With these dot products, we calculate the cross-entropy loss and use backpropagation to train the model.
elements.sent-2411-211: Inference: During inference or testing, we have access to a natural language query and the entire codebase of code snippets.
elements.sent-2411-212: To reduce inference time, we pre-compute and store the vector embeddings for each code snippet in the codebase.
elements.sent-2411-213: Then, for each natural language query we calculate the dot product between the natural language query vector and the set of pre-computed codebase vectors.
elements.sent-2411-214: These dot product results are then sorted, and the rank of the correct (``ground truth") snippet within this list is used to compute the Reciprocal Rank (Section \ref{MRR ).}
elements.sent-2411-215: Semantic Code Search results are generally evaluated using the Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR).
elements.sent-2411-216: The formula for MRR is depicted in Equation \ref{eqn:mrr_eqn , where $rank_i$ is the actual position of the ground truth snippet in the list of sorted dot products for the $i^{th}$ natural language query. \begin{equation}MRR = \frac{1}{|Q|}\sum_{i=1}^{|Q|}\frac{1}{rank_i} \label{eqn:mrr_eqn}\end{equation} The highest achievable MRR is $1$, where the ground truth is always at the $1^{st}$ position for all natural language queries.}

elements.sent-2411-238: As enterprise codebases grow in size, code maintenance eventually becomes a huge challenge.
elements.sent-2411-239: An important aspect of code maintenance is identifying and merging those code snippets that have very similar functionality.
elements.sent-2411-240: This merging allows for enhanced modularity, better consistency and higher code re-use across the entire codebase.
elements.sent-2411-241: However, manually identifying code segments that have similar functionalities can be very time consuming.
elements.sent-2411-242: Code clone detection provides a solution by predicting functionally similar code segments, irrespective of their syntactic differences.
elements.sent-2411-243: As a result, models trained on this task are particularly useful in identifying redundancies in a codebase, thereby reducing the cost of software maintenance.
elements.sent-2411-244: Code clone detection also helps in identifying plagiarism and copyright infringement in software systems \citep{Gold2021 .}

elements.sent-2411-246: Each datapoint is a pair of code snippets along with a label indicating if the two snippets are clones.

elements.sent-2411-250: As seen in Fig. \ref{fig:code_clone_detection , we fine-tune MCV Encoder on the clone detection task by running the model separately for each method in the code snippet pair.}
elements.sent-2411-251: By doing so, we produce two vectors, which we then concatenate and pass to a classification head (multi-layer perceptron).
elements.sent-2411-252: The classification head outputs a probability which along with the ground truth is used to calculate the binary cross entropy loss.
elements.sent-2411-253: During the course of training, the model learns to predict $1$ for a true clone pair and $0$ otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-254: In the inference stage, the pair of snippets are passed through MCV Encoder and if the output probability is more than $0.5$, we deem the pair to be a clone.
elements.sent-2411-255: The results of the code clone detection task are evaluated using precision, recall and F1-scores.

elements.sent-2411-273: Our third and final evaluation task is program classification.
elements.sent-2411-274: The goal of program classification is to categorize a given code snippet into different groups or classes based on the code snippet's functionality, code quality or algorithmic implementation.
elements.sent-2411-275: Using code classification techniques, we can categorize large codebases into similar groups/functionalities, allowing developers to easily navigate and manage these codebases.
elements.sent-2411-276: One common application of code classification is in software maintenance and re-engineering, where large legacy codebases need to be analyzed and restructured to meet changing business requirements \citep{BARCHI2021104075 .}
elements.sent-2411-277: Program classification serves as a tool for pinpointing components of the codebase that require extensive refactoring or complete redesign \citep{kaur2016neural .}
elements.sent-2411-278: Moreover, it can aid in highlighting potential areas of risk due to bugs or vulnerabilities \citep{8614145 .}
elements.sent-2411-279: We evaluate our performance on code classification using the Java CodeNet corpus \citep{ibmcodenet .}
elements.sent-2411-280: The dataset consists of code snippets extracted from online judge websites.
elements.sent-2411-281: Each code snippet (or code submission) solves a particular problem statement with a unique problem ID.
elements.sent-2411-282: After assigning the relevant problem ID to each code submission, we end up with a single-label multi-class dataset, which can be modelled as a classification problem.
elements.sent-2411-283: Additionally, each snippet in this dataset is a file-level snippet consisting of multiple methods and package imports.
elements.sent-2411-284: The dataset contains $75,004$ Java code snippets spread across $250$ classes.
elements.sent-2411-285: The split between train, validation and test is summarized in Table \ref{tab:classification_stats .}

elements.sent-2411-286: As shown in Fig. \ref{fig:program_classification , we fine-tune MCV Encoder on program classification using an additional classification head and then subsequently perform inference on the test set.}
elements.sent-2411-287: More specifically, we attach a compute-light classification head (multi-layer perceptron) to the MCV Encoder that takes as input a vector embedding and outputs a $250$ label array (logits).
elements.sent-2411-288: After this, we apply the softmax operation to this array and compute the cross entropy loss against the ground truth, so that the model learns to predict the correct class.
elements.sent-2411-289: During the Inference stage, we leverage our trained model in Fig. \ref{fig:program_classification to generate a probability distribution across the $250$ classes by using a softmax operation.}
elements.sent-2411-290: We then assign the class with the highest probability to the code snippet.

elements.sent-2411-308: In this section, we go over the diverse literature available for source code representation learning.
elements.sent-2411-309: We categorize most of the approaches into four broad types and elaborate on relevant works in each section:.
elements.sent-2411-310: Token-based Representation approaches such as \citep{movshovitz2013natural,allamanis2016convolutional consider code as natural language tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-311: They represent source code as a bag of words or a list of tokens, and then apply techniques like Latent Semantic Indexing (LSI) and Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA).
elements.sent-2411-312: In \citep{movshovitz2013natural , the authors predict comments from Java source files of open source projects, using topic models and n-grams.}
elements.sent-2411-313: They evaluate their methodology on the task of extreme summarization of source code, converting code snippets into short function-name-like summaries.
elements.sent-2411-314: Unlike natural languages, a lot of important information is contained in the structure of programming languages.
elements.sent-2411-315: These syntactic and semantic properties of source code can be extracted using structured or graphical representations like the AST \citep{zhang2019novel,wei2017supervised,white2016deep , CFG \citep{zhao2018deepsim , and DFG \citep{li2019improving .}}}
elements.sent-2411-316: Source code representation learning techniques of this type are grouped under Structure-based Representation approaches.
elements.sent-2411-317: Rather than using the entire AST, \citep{zhang2019novel presents a novel ASTNN approach that splits each large AST into a sequence of small statement trees, and encodes the statement trees to vectors by capturing the lexical and syntactical knowledge of statements.}
elements.sent-2411-318: They evaluated their work and saw significant improvements over the traditional, structure based approaches and the prominent clone detection tool Deckard \citep{jiang2007deckard .}
elements.sent-2411-319: Combined Representation or Multi Code-view methods attempt to combine different structured representations.
elements.sent-2411-320: These views are then combined and processed by a graph neural network (GNN) to obtain a comprehensive program representation which is evaluated in the context of the algorithm detection task using the POJ-104 and a custom and more challenging dataset called ALG-109.
elements.sent-2411-321: Most of the best results on a large variety of downstream tasks, are obtained from language models that are trained on both natural language and programming languages, drawing heavy inspiration from NLP techniques.
elements.sent-2411-322: We group these contributions \citep{feng2020codebert, nijkamp2022codegen, xu2022systematic under Sequence-based Representation approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-323: CodeBERT \citep{feng2020codebert is a pre-trained bimodal language model that learns both programming languages (PL) and natural language (NL).}
elements.sent-2411-324: CodeBERT has a Transformer-based architecture and is pre-trained on NL-PL pairs along with unimodal code data.
elements.sent-2411-325: It is performant on both natural language code search and code documentation generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-326: CodeGen, \citep{nijkamp2022codegen , a large language model for code with multi-turn program synthesis, is trained on both NL and PL data.}
elements.sent-2411-327: Their evaluation shows that the same intent provided in a multi-turn fashion significantly improves program synthesis over that provided as a single turn.
elements.sent-2411-328: Sequence-from-structure-based Representation approaches take one or more highly structured code-views (usually graphs like the AST, CFG, or DFG), and extract information as sequences by flattening them \citep{alon2019code2vec, swarna2024impact, guo2020graphcodebert .}
elements.sent-2411-329: Code2Vec is one of the pioneering works in the domain of source code representation learning where sequences were extracted from the AST of a code snippet by selecting random leaf-to-leaf paths.
elements.sent-2411-330: Using a simple neural network with a single attention layer for the model, the method was tested on the method naming task and showed a significant improvement in performance.
elements.sent-2411-331: The Mocktail approach \citep{swarna2024impact extends the path-extracting methodology of code2vec \citep{alon2019code2vec but parallelly extracts and combines paths from the AST, PDG and CFG.}}
elements.sent-2411-332: The approach is evaluated on the method naming task.
elements.sent-2411-333: The paper provides interesting insights into results obtained from word embedding and graph embedding techniques from a combined code graph.
elements.sent-2411-334: Finally, the GraphCodeBERT approach \citep{guo2020graphcodebert , achieving excellent performance on multiple tasks such as code clone detection, semantic code search, code translation and code refinement, appends data-flow sequences to the input sequence of code tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-335: The model is an extension of the CodeBERT \citep{feng2020codebert model and is pretrained on an additional task that includes data-flow information.}
elements.sent-2411-336: Closely related to CodeSAM, \citep{du2023pre proposes a directed, multiple-label code graph representation named Semantic Flow Graph (SFG) which compactly and adequately captures code semantics and achieves state-of-the-art performance in bug localization by combining it with a novel contrastive learning technique.}
elements.sent-2411-337: An interesting fused representation for vulnerability detection using carefully extended neural network models to extract vulnerability-indicative semantic features from the token sequence, attributed control flow graph (ACFG), and AST representations of a function is presented in \citep{tian2023learning .}
elements.sent-2411-338: In this section, we discuss the potential threats to the validity of our study.
elements.sent-2411-339: Internal threats - In our study, we deliberately chose a static analysis tool that operates directly on source code, enabling us to analyze even non-compilable data points.
elements.sent-2411-340: As a result, the code-views generated by the selected tool provide an approximation of control-flow and data-flow information.
elements.sent-2411-341: Therefore, findings derived from these code-views might introduce inaccuracies in our pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-342: In Section \ref{sec:experiments , for each task and dataset we have clearly outlined the techniques and modifications incorporated during data pre-processing .}
elements.sent-2411-343: While we follow established practices from existing literature, pre-processing decisions can introduce biases or limitations that may impact the study's outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-344: Another potential threat to the validity of our study could be the bias or over-fitting of our models.
elements.sent-2411-345: As we use different models for different tasks, the hyper-parameters of the model need to be appropriately tuned.
elements.sent-2411-346: Even though our models are exhaustively validated, over-fitting can occur if the data is not diverse or due to a lack of sufficient regularization strategies.
elements.sent-2411-347: External threats - Our work has been evaluated on datasets which are widely used as benchmarks for the respective SE tasks.
elements.sent-2411-348: This enables us to demonstrate the effectiveness of our models compared to existing baselines.
elements.sent-2411-349: However, the generalizability of our results is thus subject to how accurately these datasets represent the complexity of real-world code corpora.
elements.sent-2411-350: BigCloneBench, a widely accepted dataset, routinely used as a benchmark for clone detection in Java has been recently criticized for being imbalanced and biased due to the way it was constructed \citep{9978255 .}
elements.sent-2411-351: Due to a lack of a more comprehensive and well-known benchmark that fits our use case, our code clone detection evaluations are limited to BCB, in line with GraphCodeBERT which we draw comparisons against.
elements.sent-2411-352: In this work, we introduced a framework that allows researchers to infuse custom code-views into self-attention based transformer models.
elements.sent-2411-353: We propose and evaluate a novel approach to infusing information from various highly structured code-views into language models to further boost their performance.
elements.sent-2411-354: To implement this pipeline, we investigated and carefully selected the the most suitable tool that allows generating outputs tailored to our specific requirements from amongst currently available open-source options to generate code-views.
elements.sent-2411-355: We then observe the impact on performance when using various customizes combinations of code-views through this approach on the downstream SE tasks of semantic code search, code clone detection, and program classification.
elements.sent-2411-356: Our work has several important applications and extensions.
elements.sent-2411-357: The CodeSAM mechanism proposed in this work provides an exciting start-point for various language model explorations.
elements.sent-2411-358: For example, a logical extension to explore would be to pre-train a language model on a large dataset (like CodeSearchNet) using code-view inspired attention masks (like $A'$) or even code-view inspired novel pre-training objectives like ast-edge prediction and next-control-statement prediction.
elements.sent-2411-359: In addition, exploring the generalisability of the approach to more diverse SE tasks like code translation, code summarization, code optimization, and code visualization, and understanding the influence of a code-view on a specific downstream task is another line of study to research.
elements.sent-2411-360: Since the framework is flexible and highly customizable, researchers can also look into cross-language code similarity learning and its subsequent applications.
